1.synthesis: With the oracle against Tyre, Ezekiel pivots from the brief four-verse oracles against Israel’s near neighbors to a three-chapter sustained engagement with the greatest commercial power of the ancient Mediterranean. The opening oracle establishes Tyre’s crime: upon hearing of Jerusalem’s fall, Tyre said “Aha! The gate of the peoples is broken—it has swung open to me” (v. 2). The charge is commercial schadenfreude: Tyre sees Jerusalem’s role as land-bridge and trade gateway between Egypt and Mesopotamia now vacant and available for Phoenician exploitation. Where Ammon gloated over the sanctuary’s profaning and Moab denied Israel’s election, Tyre saw opportunity in Israel’s collapse—the gates that had diverted trade through Judah would now funnel commerce through Tyre. YHWH’s response introduces the sea metaphor that will dominate chs. 26–27: “I will bring many nations against you as the sea brings up its waves” (v. 3). The island fortress that trusted the sea as its moat will be overwhelmed by a sea of nations; the bare rock will become a net-drying ground.
1.historical_context: Tyre (Sur in Phoenician and Hebrew, “rock”) was built on an island roughly 700 meters off the Phoenician coast (modern Lebanon), making it nearly impregnable by land-based assault. Its natural harbors—one north, one south—made it the preeminent maritime trading hub of the eastern Mediterranean from at least the 10th century BCE through the Hellenistic period. Jerusalem’s position on the ridge between the Shephelah and the Jordan Valley gave it control over the northern trade route connecting Egypt to Damascus and Mesopotamia. Tyre’s calculation that Jerusalem’s fall opened this route to Phoenician commercial advantage was economically astute. The date of the oracle—eleventh year, first day of an unspecified month (possibly Nisan, placing it within months of Jerusalem’s fall in Tammuz/Ab 586 BCE)—reflects Ezekiel’s responsiveness to news of the city’s fate.
1.literary_structure: The oracle introduction (vv. 1–6) establishes three key structural moves that the subsequent chapters will elaborate: (1) accusation grounded in Tyre’s speech (v. 2); (2) divine counter-speech employing the sea metaphor (v. 3); (3) the bare-rock image that recurs as a refrain (vv. 4, 14). The phrase “bare rock” (sela is also the Hebrew word for the city of Petra, Edom’s capital—an ironic resonance). The inclusion of Tyre’s “daughters on the mainland” (v. 6) extends the judgment beyond the island to its satellite communities, ensuring the oracle covers the whole Tyrian commercial sphere.
1.theological_application: Tyre’s sin reveals how economic calculation can become a form of spiritual wickedness. The city did not attack Jerusalem—it simply recalculated its commercial advantage after Jerusalem fell, treating YHWH’s judgment on his people as a market opportunity. This is a form of the “Aha!” that is especially dangerous because it wears the guise of neutral economic rationality. When institutions or individuals treat the suffering of others as an opportunity for personal advancement—when another’s disaster becomes a “gate swinging open” for one’s own enrichment—they participate in Tyre’s sin. The oracle insists that commercial advantage built on another’s collapse is not neutral; it is seen, named, and judged.
1.christology: The image of YHWH sending wave upon wave of nations against Tyre anticipates the christological pattern of divine sovereignty over history. The bare rock that Tyre will become (v. 4) contrasts with the Rock on which the church is built (Matthew 16:18)—Tyre’s proud rock-island stripped bare versus the confession of the Messiah that cannot be overwhelmed. Every human commercial empire that calculates its position relative to divine judgment, treating spiritual catastrophe as market opportunity, will find its own “bare rock” judgment; the only lasting foundation is the one Tyre passed over.

7.synthesis: The siege oracle names Nebuchadnezzar explicitly as YHWH’s instrument and deploys the full vocabulary of sixth-century BCE siege warfare: earthworks (dayeq), earthen ramp (solelah), large shields (tsinah), battering rams directed at walls and towers. Nebuchadnezzar is called “king of kings” (melek melakhim)—the standard Akkadian imperial title (shar sharrani)—emphasizing that the greatest military power on earth is YHWH’s hired sword. The oracle reaches its theological climax in the “bare rock” refrain: “I will make you a bare rock; you will be a place for the spreading of nets and will never be rebuilt” (v. 14). Interpreters note a significant pronoun shift between vv. 7–11 (singular: Nebuchadnezzar) and vv. 12–13 (plural: “they”), suggesting that the full destruction of Tyre involves multiple waves of conquerors—a reading borne out historically when Nebuchadnezzar’s 13-year siege (585–573 BCE) extracted a treaty but not total destruction, and Alexander the Great in 332 BCE built a causeway from the rubble of mainland Tyre to the island and effected the final conquest.
7.historical_context: Nebuchadnezzar’s siege of Tyre (585–573 BCE) is documented in a Babylonian administrative text that records the siege’s duration. Ezekiel himself acknowledges in 29:17–20 that Nebuchadnezzar’s army received “no wages from Tyre” for its labor; the island survived and eventually capitulated to Babylonian political suzerainty without the total destruction this oracle envisions. Alexander’s 332 BCE siege completed the work: he famously demolished the ruins of mainland Tyre (the “daughters on the mainland”) and used the rubble to build a mole (causeway) to the island, then destroyed the island city. Ancient geographers after Alexander noted that fishermen dried their nets on the bare rock of old Tyre—a precise fulfillment of Ezekiel’s imagery that is remarkable regardless of one’s view of the fulfillment timeline.
7.literary_structure: The oracle builds through accumulating military images before arriving at the bare-rock refrain. The structure moves from the approaching army (v. 7) through siege operations (vv. 8–9) to the horror inside the city (vv. 10–11), then shifts to the plural conquerors who plunder and silence the city’s music (vv. 12–13), before the divine declaration of the bare rock seals the passage (v. 14). The detail of silenced music (“the sound of your harps will be heard no more”) appears again in Revelation 18:22, where it describes Babylon’s fall in language that deliberately echoes this oracle.
7.theological_application: The oracle establishes a principle fundamental to Ezekiel’s theology: political and military power, however great, is YHWH’s instrument rather than an independent force. Nebuchadnezzar is not conquering Tyre by his own might; YHWH is bringing him “from the north” as his agent. The phrase “I the LORD have spoken” (v. 14) closes with the prophetic signature of divine authority: when YHWH speaks a word, history eventually conforms to it, even when the timeline involves multiple actors across centuries. This is comfort for any community that sees powerful forces arrayed against what they understand to be God’s purposes—those forces are tools, not ultimate authorities.
7.christology: The progressive fulfillment of the Tyre oracle—from Nebuchadnezzar to Alexander to the final “no more forever”—models the pattern of prophetic fulfillment that characterizes messianic prophecy. Not every detail is fulfilled in a single historical moment; the word goes forth, partial fulfilments occur, and the full weight lands over time. The New Testament writers understand this pattern well: Revelation 18’s lament over Babylon draws directly on Ezekiel’s Tyre oracles, applying them to Rome and, typologically, to every human empire that arrays itself against the people of God. Christ’s ultimate triumph over all such powers is the final “bare rock” that reduces human pride to dust.

15.synthesis: The oracle shifts from military assault to cosmic reaction: when Tyre falls, the whole maritime world trembles. The imagery moves from battlefield to throne room: the princes of the sea descend from their thrones, remove their embroidered robes, and clothe themselves with trembling. Their lament for Tyre is formally marked as a qinah (funeral poem): “How you are destroyed, who were inhabited by seafaring men, the renowned city, who was mighty on the sea!” (v. 17). The passage captures a psychological truth about commercial interdependence: those who grew wealthy through Tyre’s trade network will not merely suffer economic loss when Tyre falls—they will be existentially shaken, because Tyre’s apparent invincibility was part of the architecture of their own security. The trembling of the coastlands at Tyre’s fall is the trembling of every system that trusted commercial power as its foundation, suddenly confronted with the fragility of what it assumed was permanent.
15.historical_context: Tyre’s commercial reach extended across the entire Mediterranean world. Phoenician trade routes ran from the Lebanese coast westward through Cyprus and Crete to Sardinia, Sicily, Carthage, and the Iberian coast (Tarshish). The city’s role as a hub made it the organizing center of international trade for centuries. The “princes of the sea” who mourn Tyre would include the rulers of Cyprus, the Aegean islands, North Africa, and Iberia—the entire Phoenician commercial sphere. The qinah meter (3:2 stress pattern) is the Hebrew funeral meter, used elsewhere for the death of individuals (Amos 5:1–2) and cities (Lamentations 1:1). Applying it to Tyre gives her fall the formal gravity of a royal funeral.
15.literary_structure: This section (vv. 15–18) introduces the formal lament structure that will dominate chs. 27 and 28. The movement is spatial (from the siege of vv. 7–14 to the global reaction here) and modal (from proclamation of judgment to quotation of the mourners’ lament). The quoted qinah in v. 17 is the first of several embedded laments in the Tyre cycle; 27:32–36 and 28:12–19 extend the same mourning form. The shift from “the LORD will bring” to “the coastlands tremble” moves from divine action to human response, showing that YHWH’s judgment is witnessed and felt across the geopolitical world.
15.theological_application: The trembling of the sea-princes at Tyre’s fall illustrates what Augustine called the restlessness of the heart founded on anything other than God. The maritime rulers who built their security on Tyre’s permanence find that permanence was illusory; their trembling is the symptom of misplaced trust. The oracle challenges communities that anchor their sense of security in commercial power, political alliances, or the stability of dominant institutions. When those institutions fall—as Tyre fell, as Rome fell—those who depended on them are left with the existential terror that this section captures: the robes removed, the trembling put on. Only those whose security rests on YHWH rather than on commercial empires escape this kind of cosmic vertigo.
15.christology: The nations’ trembling at Tyre’s fall is a dim foreshadowing of the eschatological trembling of Revelation 18:9–10, where the kings who committed adultery with Babylon weep and mourn when they see her burning. John’s language is drawn directly from Ezekiel 26–27: the same mourning princes, the same lament over a fallen commercial empire. But where Tyre’s fall brought only trembling, Babylon’s fall in Revelation is followed immediately by the hallelujah chorus of Revelation 19:1–5—because Babylon’s fall is the precondition for the marriage supper of the Lamb. The nations’ grief becomes the church’s joy.

19.pericope_label: Tyre Will Go Down to the Pit
19.synthesis: The final movement of the Tyre introduction oracle descends into cosmic mythology. YHWH threatens to make Tyre “a desolate city like cities that are not inhabited,” then to “bring up the deep (tehom) over you and the great waters will cover you” (v. 19). The island city, which used the sea as its moat and its commercial highway, will be submerged by the primordial deep—an anti-creation image of returning to the chaos beneath the ordered world. Then YHWH will “bring you down with those who descend to the Pit (bor), to the people of old” (v. 20). The descent to the Pit is the descent to the underworld, to the silent realm of the dead who preceded Tyre in history. The oracle closes with terrifying finality: “you will be sought but will never be found again, declares the Lord GOD” (v. 21). Not merely destroyed but erased from the cosmos’ record—“no more forever” (lo timmatsei od le’olam).
19.historical_context: The cosmic imagery of the deep (tehom) covering Tyre invokes the ancient Near Eastern cosmological vocabulary of Chaoskampf: the primordial waters that existed before creation, held back by YHWH’s ordering act (Genesis 1:2). To have the deep swallow a city is to reverse creation—to unmake it. The descent to the “people of old” in the Pit resonates with ANE underworld literature: the Sumerian “Descent of Inanna” and the Babylonian “Descent of Ishtar” both describe a realm of ancient dead rulers. Ezekiel develops this motif most fully in Ezekiel 32:17–32, where Egypt joins Assyria, Elam, Meshech, Tubal, and Edom in the underworld assembly. The oracle’s rhetorical power lies in its claim that Tyre’s end is not merely political defeat but cosmic annihilation.
19.literary_structure: Three verses conclude the chapter with an eschatological cadence. The three movements are parallel: (1) cosmic waters cover the city (v. 19); (2) descent to the Pit with the ancient dead, leaving the living world to the restored (v. 20); (3) terror and erasure (v. 21). The phrase “I will place you in the depths of the earth, in places desolate from of old (meolam)” (v. 20) forms a contrast with “no more forever (le’olam)” (v. 21)—the same root word (olam, eternity/hidden time) brackets Tyre’s descent: ancient desolation receives her; eternal absence is her fate.
19.theological_application: The descent to the Pit is not merely poetic hyperbole; it represents the ultimate futility of building one’s civilization on commercial power without acknowledgment of YHWH. The city that will be “sought but never found” is the city whose entire legacy leaves no permanent trace. Against this, the oracle implicitly places YHWH’s city, whose foundations are described in Psalm 87:1 as “on the holy mountain”—Jerusalem, which YHWH will restore even after its own fall, unlike Tyre. The contrast warns that not all cities have the same eschatological trajectory: some are built to endure; others are built for the Pit.
19.christology: The “no more forever” of Tyre’s annihilation stands in stark contrast to the “forever and ever” of Christ’s kingdom (Revelation 11:15). Where Tyre descends to the Pit with the ancient dead, Christ descended to the dead and rose, breaking the Pit’s hold (Acts 2:31; Revelation 1:18: “I hold the keys of Death and Hades”). The cosmic waters that cover Tyre are the same primordial forces over which Christ walked (Matthew 14:25)—his lordship over sea and death undoes the doom that swallows Tyre.
